title: SlabVault graded-card Shopify batch: background removal onto clean white, straighten, center, and 2048x2048 export

source.reference: Shopify Product Photo Editor — Background Removal & Centering

grounding_note: VERBATIM from the Upwork brief: an online sports-card store owner needs a photo editor to process product images of "trading cards, graded PSA slabs, sealed hobby boxes" that were "taken on iPhone" — the job is to "Remove background from product photos", "Center the product on a clean white or transparent background", "Resize and export to Shopify's recommended dimensions (2048 x 2048px)", "Deliver files named to match Shopify product handles for easy upload", and "Maintain a consistent look across all product images", with the hard requirement that "cards must be straight, centered, no shadows" because "these are collectibles worth" real money so attention to detail matters. REASONABLY ENRICHED into a runnable Adobe-only benchmark: the real brief is a per-item cutout-and-center-on-white pipeline, which the connector palette genuinely supports (image_select_subject to inspect the cutout region, image_remove_background to lift the product both onto transparent PNG AND onto a solid pure-white background in one sanctioned call, image_auto_straighten to level the tilt, image_apply_auto_tone / image_adjust_color_temperature to fix the dull cool kitchen cast, image_crop_and_resize to center and square to exactly 2048x2048). The fictional store SlabVault Cards models the pushingpacks.com sports-card shop; the six slab/box SKUs, the Shopify product handles, and the shared brand-consistency requirement are drawn straight from the brief. No deliverable was invented beyond the Shopify-ready white-background product set the brief asks for; the CSV of Shopify handles honors the literal "named to match Shopify product handles" instruction and drives per-file naming.
one_line_ask: Cut six iPhone-shot graded trading-card / hobby-box photos out of their cluttered backgrounds, straighten and tone them, place each on a clean pure-white background, and export a consistent 2048x2048 square set (plus transparent PNG cutouts) named to match the store's Shopify product handles.
full_brief: SlabVault Cards, an online collectibles store selling graded slabs and sealed hobby product, needs its new-inventory photos turned into clean, upload-ready Shopify images. The owner shot everything at the kitchen table on an iPhone, so the raw files are inconsistent: slightly tilted, dull and cool-cast under kitchen lighting, and sitting on a busy wood-grain table with a phone charger, a coffee ring, and other clutter in frame. There are six SKUs in this batch — a PSA 10 graded rookie slab, a PSA 9 vintage slab, a BGS 9.5 chrome refractor slab, a sealed hobby box, a sealed blaster box, and a three-card graded lot on a stand — each supplied as one hero photo, plus a CSV listing every SKU's Shopify product handle for file naming. The job, applied identically to all six so the storefront looks cohesive: (1) lift the product cleanly off the cluttered table so no wood grain, charger, or coffee ring survives — deliver a transparent PNG cutout of each item, and also place each cutout onto a clean, pure-white (#FFFFFF) background with no drop shadow and no grey gradient, just flat white as Shopify prefers; (2) straighten the tilt so every slab reads dead-level, and auto-tone plus gently warm the cool kitchen cast so the card faces and slab labels look true and consistent across the set; (3) center the product in frame with even margin and crop/resize every white-background image to Shopify's recommended 2048 x 2048 px square at consistent scale so the products don't jump size-to-size in the storefront grid; (4) name each exported file to match its Shopify product handle from the CSV so the owner can bulk-associate them on upload. Deliver the six finished 2048x2048 white-background JPEGs, keep the transparent PNG cutouts too (useful for marketplace cross-posting), stage everything into a dated Shopify-upload folder in Creative Cloud, and drop a short recipe note documenting the exact edit steps so the same look can be reproduced on the next inventory drop. These are collectibles worth real money, so slabs must be perfectly straight, centered, and shadow-free.

inputs.name: sku_psa10_rookie_slab.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: A modern baseball rookie trading card sealed in a clear hard-plastic grading slab; the slab's top label reads 'GRADEX  GEM-MT 10' in red on white; the rookie card and its photo are clearly visible and readable, no glare hiding it, resting slightly tilted on a plain light-wood desk. Shot like a real trading-card seller's clean phone photo for an online listing: the item is CENTERED, SHARP, well-lit under soft even daylight and fills most of the frame — clearly the subject. A plain, simple surface with only a hint of casual context (to be cut out later). Realistic and neat — NOT messy, NOT cluttered, NO random junk. Any grading label shows a FICTIONAL grading company, NEVER a real brand (no PSA, BGS, SGC, CGC). It is a REAL PHOTOGRAPH — not an illustration or 3D render.
inputs.realism_notes: Deliberately imperfect (tilt, cool cast, busy table, plastic glare) so the cutout + straighten + tone + center-on-white pipeline has genuine work to do; gpt-image-2 for photoreal reflective plastic.

inputs.name: sku_psa9_vintage_slab.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: An older 1970s-style vintage baseball trading card sealed in a clear grading slab with a label reading 'GRADEX  NM-MT 9'; the vintage card is CLEARLY VISIBLE and readable with NO glare obscuring it, resting flat and centered on a plain wood desk, soft window light. Shot like a real trading-card seller's clean phone photo for an online listing: the item is CENTERED, SHARP, well-lit under soft even daylight and fills most of the frame — clearly the subject. A plain, simple surface with only a hint of casual context (to be cut out later). Realistic and neat — NOT messy, NOT cluttered, NO random junk. Any grading label shows a FICTIONAL grading company, NEVER a real brand (no PSA, BGS, SGC, CGC). It is a REAL PHOTOGRAPH — not an illustration or 3D render.
inputs.realism_notes: Second SKU with the same imperfections so all six can be graded to one consistent look; underexposure justifies auto-tone.

inputs.name: sku_bgs95_refractor_slab.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: A shiny chrome-refractor basketball trading card sealed in a grading slab with a black label reading 'APEX  9.5' and four small subgrade numbers; the colourful refractor card is clearly visible and sharp, centered on a plain neutral surface. Shot like a real trading-card seller's clean phone photo for an online listing: the item is CENTERED, SHARP, well-lit under soft even daylight and fills most of the frame — clearly the subject. A plain, simple surface with only a hint of casual context (to be cut out later). Realistic and neat — NOT messy, NOT cluttered, NO random junk. Any grading label shows a FICTIONAL grading company, NEVER a real brand (no PSA, BGS, SGC, CGC). It is a REAL PHOTOGRAPH — not an illustration or 3D render.
inputs.realism_notes: Reflective refractor + busy background stress the subject-select and background-removal steps; consistent look target across the set.

inputs.name: sku_sealed_hobby_box.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: A sealed trading-card hobby box, a clean rectangular cardboard box with shrink-wrap sheen and a foil top strip, fictional brand 'STARFALL', standing upright and centered on a plain light counter, evenly lit, clearly the subject. Shot like a real trading-card seller's clean phone photo for an online listing: the item is CENTERED, SHARP, well-lit under soft even daylight and fills most of the frame — clearly the subject. A plain, simple surface with only a hint of casual context (to be cut out later). Realistic and neat — NOT messy, NOT cluttered, NO random junk. Any grading label shows a FICTIONAL grading company, NEVER a real brand (no PSA, BGS, SGC, CGC). It is a REAL PHOTOGRAPH — not an illustration or 3D render.
inputs.realism_notes: Larger 3D box (not a flat slab) so the crop/center must handle a taller subject at consistent scale; shrink-wrap glare tests the cutout.

inputs.name: sku_sealed_blaster_box.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: A small sealed retail 'blaster' box of trading cards, brightly printed fictional brand, shrink-wrapped, standing upright and centered on a plain wood surface, evenly lit. Shot like a real trading-card seller's clean phone photo for an online listing: the item is CENTERED, SHARP, well-lit under soft even daylight and fills most of the frame — clearly the subject. A plain, simple surface with only a hint of casual context (to be cut out later). Realistic and neat — NOT messy, NOT cluttered, NO random junk. Any grading label shows a FICTIONAL grading company, NEVER a real brand (no PSA, BGS, SGC, CGC). It is a REAL PHOTOGRAPH — not an illustration or 3D render.
inputs.realism_notes: Most-tilted SKU so image_auto_straighten has clear work; retail-print box tests consistent white-background centering.

inputs.name: sku_graded_lot_stand.jpg
inputs.gen_prompt: Three graded trading-card slabs propped upright in a small clear acrylic display stand as a lot; all three cards are clearly visible and sharp, the group centered on a plain surface under soft daylight. Shot like a real trading-card seller's clean phone photo for an online listing: the item is CENTERED, SHARP, well-lit under soft even daylight and fills most of the frame — clearly the subject. A plain, simple surface with only a hint of casual context (to be cut out later). Realistic and neat — NOT messy, NOT cluttered, NO random junk. Any grading label shows a FICTIONAL grading company, NEVER a real brand (no PSA, BGS, SGC, CGC). It is a REAL PHOTOGRAPH — not an illustration or 3D render.
inputs.realism_notes: Multi-object subject (three slabs on a stand) makes the subject selection and clean cutout genuinely harder; same grade/center/crop target.

inputs.name: shopify_handles.csv
inputs.gen_prompt: Generate a CSV with columns source_file,shopify_handle,title mapping each of the six SKU photos to its Shopify product handle and a short title, e.g. sku_psa10_rookie_slab.jpg,psa-10-rookie-slab,"PSA 10 Rookie Slab". Handles must be lowercase, hyphenated, URL-safe, one row per source file, matching the six supplied filenames exactly.
inputs.realism_notes: Honors the brief's literal 'Deliver files named to match Shopify product handles' requirement; drives per-file output naming, not a layout data-merge.

outputs.name: shopify_whitebg_squares_x6
outputs.spec: Six 2048x2048 px JPEGs, each product composited onto a flat pure-white (#FFFFFF) background, straightened, tone-matched and centered at consistent scale, no drop shadow, named to match the Shopify product handle from the CSV (e.g. psa-10-rookie-slab.jpg).

outputs.name: transparent_cutouts_x6
outputs.spec: Six transparent-background PNG cutouts of the same products (background removed, straightened, toned) retained for marketplace cross-posting; same handle-based naming.

outputs.name: SlabVault_Shopify_Upload_folder
outputs.spec: A dated Creative Cloud folder (e.g. SlabVault_Shopify_2026-07-01) containing the six white-background JPEGs and six transparent PNGs, staged for bulk Shopify upload.

outputs.name: slabvault_edit_recipe.txt
outputs.spec: A short recipe note documenting the exact per-image edit steps (straighten, auto-tone, warm, background-remove to transparent, background-remove onto #FFFFFF white, center-crop to 2048x2048, handle naming) so the same consistent look reproduces on the next inventory drop.

connector_workflow.note: Create the dated SlabVault_Shopify_2026-07-01 Creative Cloud folder to stage all finished upload-ready assets.
Level the ~5-degree tilt on the PSA 10 hero so the slab reads dead-straight for the collectibles-grade look (done before cutout so the whole frame rotates cleanly).
Auto-tone to fix the dull/flat cool kitchen exposure so the card face and PSA label read true.
Warm the cool bluish white balance toward neutral so colors are consistent across the batch (same shift reused on all six).
Detect and preview the slab subject region to confirm a clean cutout is possible off the cluttered wood table / charger / coffee ring before removal (inspection only).
Remove the cluttered background to a transparent PNG cutout of the graded, straightened slab (default no-backgroundColor mode) — retained deliverable for marketplace cross-posting.

Second removal pass on the same graded frame with options.backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' — the tool-sanctioned single-call 'product on clean white' path — compositing the slab onto flat pure-white with no shadow, no gradient (not image_fill_area).

Center the product (focus:subject) and crop/resize to Shopify's 2048x2048 square with even margin at the consistent batch scale; onSubjectClipping set to 'pad' (never 'expand') so no generative-expand chain is ever triggered.
Run the identical pipeline on the PSA 9 vintage slab: level the ~3-degree tilt first.
Auto-tone the underexposed vintage slab to match the set.
Remove background to a transparent PNG cutout of the vintage slab (retained deliverable).
Second pass with backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' to place the vintage-slab cutout on clean flat white.
Center and crop/resize the vintage slab to the matching 2048x2048 square (onSubjectClipping:'pad').
Straighten the reflective BGS refractor slab shot.
Auto-tone the dull refractor slab to the consistent brightness.
Remove background to a clean transparent PNG cutout of the reflective refractor slab (chrome glare / busy background handled by the cutout).
Second pass with backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' to place the refractor cutout on clean flat white.
Center and crop/resize the refractor slab to 2048x2048 (onSubjectClipping:'pad').
Straighten the taller 3D sealed hobby box shot.
Auto-tone the hobby-box shot to the batch look.
Remove background to a transparent PNG of the hobby box (shrink-wrap glare handled by the cutout).
Second pass with backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' to place the hobby-box cutout on clean flat white.
Center the taller box and crop/resize to 2048x2048 at consistent scale so it doesn't dwarf the slabs in the grid (onSubjectClipping:'pad').
Straighten the most-tilted (~6-degree) sealed blaster box so it reads level.
Auto-tone the blaster-box shot to the consistent exposure.
Remove background to a transparent PNG cutout of the blaster box (retained deliverable).
Second pass with backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' to place the blaster-box cutout on clean flat white.
Center and crop/resize the blaster box to 2048x2048 (onSubjectClipping:'pad').
Prompt-select 'the three graded slabs in the acrylic stand' to preview/confirm the multi-object product group holds together as one clean cutout region before removal (inspection only).
Straighten the three-slab lot-on-stand shot.
Auto-tone the graded-lot shot to the batch look.
Remove background to a transparent PNG of the three-slab lot on its stand (retained deliverable).
Second pass with backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' to place the three-slab-lot cutout on clean flat white.
Center and crop/resize the graded lot to the final 2048x2048 square (onSubjectClipping:'pad'), completing the consistent six-image set.

connector_workflow.inputs_from: psa10_square_2048, psa9_square_2048, bgs95_square_2048, hobbybox_square_2048, blaster_square_2048, lot_square_2048, psa10_transparent_png, psa9_transparent_png, bgs95_transparent_png, hobbybox_transparent_png, blaster_transparent_png, lot_transparent_png
connector_workflow.note: Initialize an upload session for all twelve finished assets (six white-background JPEGs + six transparent PNGs).
Finalize the upload into the dated Shopify folder, naming each file per its Shopify product handle from the CSV.
Emit a recipe note documenting the exact per-image edit steps and the handle-naming map so the same consistent white-background look reproduces on the next inventory drop.

chaining_note: Strict output-to-input chaining throughout. Each of the six SKUs runs its own grade-then-cutout-onto-white sub-chain, and every step consumes only the prior step's output (or the raw SKU photo that seeds its sub-chain). The PSA 10 hero runs the fullest pipeline: sku_psa10_rookie_slab.jpg -> auto_straighten(2) -> auto_tone(3) -> color_temperature(4); the graded frame (psa10_warmed) then feeds an inspect step select_subject(5) and TWO background removals off the same frame — remove_background default(6) yields the transparent PNG cutout, remove_background with backgroundColor '#FFFFFF'(7) yields the on-white composite — and the on-white feeds crop_and_resize 2048(8). The PSA 9 (9-13), BGS refractor (14-18), hobby box (19-23) and blaster box (24-28) each repeat the straighten->tone->remove_bg(transparent)->remove_bg(on-white)->crop sub-chain; the graded lot (29-34) adds an image_select_by_prompt inspect(29) to confirm the three-slab multi-object group holds as one cutout. All twelve finished outputs (six *_square_2048 JPEGs + six *_transparent_png cutouts) converge into the upload session(35) -> finalize(36), which also consumes the dated folder from step 1 and shopify_handles.csv for per-file naming. The recipe note(37) closes the chain, consuming the finalized asset URNs and the handles CSV. Nothing after the raw SKU loads originates outside the chain; the finished Shopify set emerges only from the assembled pipeline.
difficulty_rationale: Long-horizon batch: 37 connector calls, 10 distinct Adobe tools across three groups (image_* raster editing, asset_* storage/naming, local recipe note) — well above the >=10 calls / >=6 distinct-tool floor. Difficulty comes from running six separate SKUs (flat slabs, a reflective refractor, tall 3D boxes, a multi-object lot) through a consistent straighten -> auto-tone -> warm -> background-removal -> center-crop pipeline, holding scale and white-balance consistency across the whole set, branching each item into BOTH a transparent PNG cutout and a clean-white composite, then naming every export to its Shopify handle from a supplied CSV. It respects every hard limit: 'product on clean white' is produced the tool-sanctioned way via image_remove_background with options.backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' (a single call), NOT by feeding a transparent PNG into image_fill_area (which the tool docs explicitly warn against — wrong mask polarity — and which is the reason image_fill_area is deliberately unused here); image_crop_and_resize sets onSubjectClipping to 'pad' so it never emits a requires_expand_chain / image_crop_and_resize directive; no generative fill / generative-expand / reframe-crop, no text-to-image / from-scratch art, no upscaling, no object-removal-with-reconstruction; image_select_subject and image_select_by_prompt only INSPECT/preview the cutout region, they do not synthesize. Every image step is a whole-image or honest cutout operation, exactly the real freelance cutout-on-white job.

reverify.banned_capability_check: CONFIRMED none of the banned capabilities are used: no image_crop_and_resize / reframe-crop / reframe-crop (image_crop_and_resize is set to onSubjectClipping='pad', which suppresses the requires_expand_chain fallback so no image_crop_and_resize directive is ever emitted); no generative fill of new content; no text-to-image or from-scratch art/logo; no upscaling / super-resolution; no object-removal-with-reconstruction (inpainting); no bespoke font design; no unreachable external account. image_remove_background is used only (a) in default mode to produce transparent PNG cutouts and (b) with options.backgroundColor='#FFFFFF' to composite the SAME cut-out subject onto flat white — both are honest cutout/backdrop operations, not pixel synthesis. image_fill_area is deliberately NOT used: the authored draft chained image_remove_background's transparent PNG into image_fill_area, which the tool's own docs forbid (transparent PNG has alpha, not black/white mask semantics -> wrong polarity) and which is the wrong path for 'entire background behind a subject on white' — that defect is corrected here by using image_remove_background's backgroundColor option instead.
reverify.notes: Each potentially-risky step stays in-bounds: (1) image_remove_background transparent pass (n6,11,16,21,26,32) simply drops the cluttered table to transparent using auto subject detection — a cutout, no new pixels. (2) image_remove_background on-white pass (n7,12,17,22,27,33) composites the same detected subject onto solid #FFFFFF via the tool's sanctioned backgroundColor option (its documented 'product shot on white' path) — flat white, no shadow, no gradient, honoring the brief; it is NOT generative fill and does NOT reconstruct occluded pixels. (3) image_select_subject / image_select_by_prompt (n5,29) only preview/confirm the cutout region; they inspect, they do not generate. (4) image_crop_and_resize (n8,13,18,23,28,34) reframes/centers to 2048x2048 by cropping and downscaling existing pixels with onSubjectClipping='pad' (never 'expand'), so no upscaling and no generative-expand fallback is ever invoked. (5) image_auto_straighten / image_apply_auto_tone / image_adjust_color_temperature are whole-image tonal/geometric corrections. (6) local_write_recipe_note (n37) is a local [L] documentation step. (7) asset_* steps are storage/folder/naming only. The full chain is the genuine, fully-doable freelance 'remove background, center on clean white, resize to Shopify 2048x2048 square, name to handles' job — feasible end to end with the allowed palette.
